documents.title: University Policy 1.20 - Use of Generative Artificial Intelligence Tools

ai_strategy.summary: Michigan Tech combines formal University Policy 1.20 on generative AI, an Academic Affairs AI site, Google Gemini access through MTU accounts, and a Center for Artificial Intelligence with 30 faculty and more than $12.6 million in affiliated awards since FY22. Its public posture is responsible-use plus research-strengthening.

key_provisions: Use AI in accordance with academic integrity expectations and instructor or unit guidance
Do not enter restricted, confidential, regulated, or sensitive data into unapproved AI tools
Review AI outputs for accuracy, hallucination, bias, copyright, and attribution issues
Disclose or cite AI use when required by course, research, publisher, or unit rules
Keep human judgment and accountability in consequential academic, research, and administrative decisions
ai_policy.summary: Michigan Technological University's AI policy posture is best characterized as comprehensive: public sources show explicit expectations for responsible use, privacy, disclosure, academic integrity, and human review, while leaving some implementation decisions to units or instructors.

bodies.name: AI Working Group at Michigan Tech
bodies.scope: Academic Affairs group supporting policy, pedagogy, FAQs, and professional guidance.

governance_structure.summary: Michigan Technological University's public AI governance is organized through AI Working Group at Michigan Tech, Center for Artificial Intelligence.

initiatives.description: Michigan Tech's policy provides high-level guidance for affiliates using AI tools and requires disclosure when AI is a significant component of work product.

research_centers.name: Center for Artificial Intelligence
research_centers.focus: Interdisciplinary AI research in transportation, materials design, health informatics, climate resiliency, and trustworthy AI.

computing_infrastructure.summary: Michigan Tech's Superior shared HPC infrastructure is available to all researchers and provides the central research-computing backbone. A public Applied Computing GPU cluster configuration also describes five GPU servers for AI, deep learning, HPC, data science, visualization, and life-science work, but the current centralized AI GPU inventory was not published as a single count.

equity_and_access_initiatives.title: AI policy disclosure requirement
equity_and_access_initiatives.description: Policy 1.20 requires disclosure of significant AI-facilitated content and frames reputation, IP, equity, and privacy risks.

regulatory_context.summary: Michigan Technological University operates in Michigan; public AI guidance should be read alongside applicable state, federal, and institutional privacy, security, accessibility, and academic-integrity requirements.

narrative_summary: Michigan Technological University (Houghton, MI) is an R1 institution whose public AI posture shows activity across governance, teaching, research, enterprise tooling, workforce readiness, and access. Michigan Tech combines formal University Policy 1.20 on generative AI, an Academic Affairs AI site, Google Gemini access through MTU accounts, and a Center for Artificial Intelligence with 30 faculty and more than $12.6 million in affiliated awards since FY22. Its public posture is responsible-use plus research-strengthening. The most concrete public initiatives captured here are University Policy 1.20 for generative AI; Google Gemini access through MTU accounts; Center for Artificial Intelligence; Artificial Intelligence at Michigan Tech academic initiative; Proposed B.S. in Artificial Intelligence. Across the profile, the strongest evidence comes from official university AI hubs, policy pages, teaching resources, research-center pages, and program pages rather than secondary reporting. The institution_type is set to r1_research from the canonical Carnegie R1 list. Known gaps are intentionally explicit: leadership names, user counts, detailed budgets, and compute counts are only included when they surfaced in public sources. Overall, the profile supports at least five AI initiatives and evidences strategy/leadership, governance, teaching/curriculum, research infrastructure, student services or enterprise tooling, workforce/community, partnerships, and equity dimensions where public sources permit.
